natural_occurrence.description: Bright red carotenoid pigment that gives tomatoes their characteristic red color. Lycopene has eleven conjugated double bonds, which contribute to its deep red hue and antioxidant properties.

manufacturing.description: E160d includes three types: (i) synthetic lycopene produced by chemical synthesis; (ii) lycopene extract from tomatoes using solvent extraction; and (iii) lycopene from Blakeslea trispora fungus through fermentation. Commercial food-grade lycopene is typically extracted from tomato processing by-products.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used as a colorant and antioxidant in cosmetics.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used in dietary supplements and nutraceuticals.

eu.conditions: Approved with three sub-types: E160d(i) synthetic, E160d(ii) tomato extract, E160d(iii) from Blakeslea trispora

usa.notes: Approved for use in foods generally

japan.notes: Approved as tomato pigment (natural lycopene from tomatoes); synthetic lycopene is distinguished separately

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: ADI not specified (for lycopene from all sources)

food_categories_eu.category_name: Fat emulsions mainly of type water-in-oil

commonly_found_in: Tomato soup and canned tomato products
fun_facts: Lycopene is what makes tomatoes red - the deeper the red color, the higher the lycopene content.
Processed tomato products like ketchup contain more bioavailable lycopene (9.9-13.44 mg/100g) than raw tomatoes (0.88-7.74 mg/100g).
Heating tomatoes and combining them with oil increases lycopene absorption by the body.
Lycopene has been studied for its potential cardiovascular benefits, though evidence from studies shows conflicting results.
As a carotenoid, lycopene is fat-soluble, meaning it is better absorbed when consumed with dietary fats.
health_benefits_disclaimer: While lycopene has been studied for various health properties including antioxidant effects, lycopene as a food colorant (E160d) is used in very small quantities. Research on lycopene's health effects has shown mixed results. Any health-related information should not be considered medical advice. Consult healthcare professionals for personalized health guidance.
